claim: There are only two skills you have to have in life: How to build and how to sell.
speaker: Nivi

claim: Today we’re going to talk about the sales side.
speaker: Nivi

claim: Naval is a particularly gifted seller, and some people have accused him of having his own reality distortion field.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I just try to figure things out, and then if there’s something I believe in, I try to convey that as accurately and honestly as possible to the other person.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think the full extent of my sales training is that I watched Glengarry Glen Ross.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I read Robert Cialdini’s Influence—his original book on persuasion.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It has one good idea in there, which is anchoring, but that’s it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Robert Cialdini popularized CLASSR—which is consistency, liking, authority, scarcity, social proof, and reciprocity.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Those are like the six ways in which you influence people.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then in his subsequent book he added a seventh, which was basically anchoring.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you feel like someone is selling to you, and if you feel like you’re being sold, it’s a turnoff.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Humans are hardwired to resist being sold to.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I think what matters much more is credibility.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Credibility is way more important than sales.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you want to be good at “sales,” then really what you want is you want to be credible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You want to be the real estate agent that steers people away from bad deals and bad neighborhoods and bad houses, so that when the right one comes along for them, they trust you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You really have to take your ego out of it, put yourself in the other person’s shoes and figure out what they want.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
And then once you’ve figured out what they want, then you can “make the sale.”

But I’m not the guy you would talk to for making the sales quota, where you have to sell like 50 pieces of software this week or this month.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But what I can tell you is that the people you most want to impress in life are the ones who can see right through you.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And they will see right through your sales tactics; they will see right through your pitching them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you want to work with the people who are at the top of the top, if you want to successfully sell the biggest things that are out there, then you have to be very credible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And to be credible, you have to be authentic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They have to be able to trust you, and that you’re not just doing what’s expedient for you—you’re thinking about the long term.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You understand what you’re talking about, and you can explain it simply.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: They’re not going to come up to speed quickly enough, so you do have to figure out how to analogize it and convey it to them, and it doesn’t always work.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I know there is a model out there that does work, where some people are incredibly aggressive.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Just keep beating on it, and eventually you’ll break through.”

I’m not one of those people—I’m just lazy.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I like to go where it’s easy, so if my “sale,” or if my pitch does not resonate with somebody, I move on.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I move on instantly, and I just keep looking for the person whom it will resonate with.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: That’s a technique, and there are times when I do contradict people outright.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And it’s selfish honesty because I want to make the right decisions for myself, which means that I have to try to be as objective as possible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I’d just be walking around with blinders on and probably be wrong 95% of the time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The next technique I’ve noticed in Naval is that he is truthful and positive at the same time.
speaker: Nivi

claim: If you ask me for advice, I can be truthful or I can be positive, but it’s incredibly difficult, and it’s a really rare skill to be able to do both at the same time.
speaker: Nivi

claim: It takes a lot for me to fire someone, and I’ve gotten better at it over time, but I’ve historically been very bad at it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: In most companies I’ll lean on my co-founders to be the ones doing that.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So when it’s time to fire someone, even though they might be a drag on the team or it’s not the right place for them, I’m empathic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And really the only way I’ve found to fire people that works for me is I have to be convinced that they’re going to be more effective somewhere else in some other role, and then I have to help them find that role or at least be available as a reference for them on that role.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: By the way, when I say truthful and positive, what I mean by positive is that you leave the conversation with Naval energized to actually work on the discussion you just had.
speaker: Nivi

claim: And I think leadership is one of the core, core things that you have to drive in an organization.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You just have to inspire people to want to do the work.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you do have to take the time to listen to what those people want, and then figure out where there’s an overlap in what you want done, and then inspire them to go do it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Instead, teach them to yearn for the vast and endless sea.”

So if I’m recruiting someone, I’m always pitching them on what I think is correct—it’s honest; it’s authentic—which is that startups are just a much better way to build businesses, to live your life, and much more fun to work in than large companies.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And yes, in the short term it’s grueling, but in the long term, once you’ve done startups, it’s very difficult to go work for a big company.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Once you have worked with a lot of autonomy or worked for yourself, it’s very hard to be employable in a traditional sense.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I think one of my most popular tweets was, “A taste of freedom can make you unemployable.” And that’s kind of what I mean, especially for talented and skilled people who are self-motivated.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And you’re not going to do your best work when you’re not free.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you want to be in the minimum-sized group to do the scope of the job that you set out to do.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: We are designed to go on missions with small groups: everything from people jumping on a boat and sailing to the West Indies or to the New World, or people going to war, or people going to build a business.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So when you read these stories like Liftoff, which is a story of Musk and his team building the first SpaceX rocket, or The Macintosh Way, which is about Jobs and his team building the first Macintosh—and there are other various books, like there’s Soul of a New Machine, etc.—these are incredibly inspiring.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Because I think deep, deep, deep down, this is what every person really craves, which is if you’re building something, you’re in that kind of world where you want to be with a small team of highly competent people, each pulling their weight and doing their job to the best, such that you can completely rely on them, and then you get to do your creativity at the max.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then at the other side, you get this incredible outcome.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And that’s more of the model in normal society.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you see low trust societies, where people don’t trust each other, or where the structure makes it impossible to work together—the taxes are too high, the regulations are too high, there’s too much bureaucracy—things are illegal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: People know each other in the social network; they can check each other out.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then you can get together these teams of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 people who can do what was considered impossible.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You want to function with high-trust people in small groups to solve really hard problems.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And so when I’m “pitching” people on joining a company, that’s what I’m pitching.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
This is where you have the most fun.”

One of the recruiting tricks that I use is—you’ve got to keep the bar super high.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The next technique I’ve noticed is that you don’t sell anything that you’re not inspired about.
speaker: Nivi

claim: I asked Jeff Fagnan from Accomplice—a friend of ours— what he thinks about your sales skills, and he described it as an “evangelical sale.”
speaker: Nivi

claim: If you’re not excited about the thing, what are you doing selling it?
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: It’s a miserable life if you’re selling things that you don’t care about.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
I know in The Wolf of Wall Street and all those kinds of movies, it’s lionized like, “Hey, sell me this pen.”

You know, “Sell me this fork.”

I don’t care about selling you this pen or selling you this fork, unless I believe it’s the best pen ever made.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If I believe it’s the best pen ever made, I’ll sell it for free.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So I think for me, sales is a byproduct of credibility, and obviously it helps to be articulate and it helps to be empathic.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: In terms of the actual sales skills, I know the whole Cialdini checklist.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: All frameworks in life—whether it’s like your working out framework or your sales framework, or even your building an app framework—they’re all secondary, and distant secondary, to your motivation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: One of the reasons why I think my cryptic tweets work better than formulas is because they’re really about inspiration and motivation.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If you’re the kind of person who needs to read motivational tweets and listen to motivational podcasts all the time, then you’re probably not going to make it either, because you have to be self-motivated at some level.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if that fire can’t be lit, then you’re just going to be listening to motivational podcasts until the end of time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So closing the loop on vibe coding—vibe coding is just my latest obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Every six months I get obsessed with something new, and I’ve learned over time, there are bad obsessions—like, if you are overeating, or if you’re doing drugs, or you’re playing too many video games, or anything like that—that’s a bad obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If I’m into some piece of technology, if I’m loving some podcast where I might be learning something, if I’m getting into some specific kind of workout, I feed that obsession.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And then once the obsession phase passes, like it inevitably does—you get a little tired of the thing—some large piece of it stays with you for the rest of your life, and you just learn how to work it into your everyday psyche and personality and being.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Like if you want to be good at sales, then find something that you really care about that you want to sell and go sell that thing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If it feels to you like you’re selling, then you’re probably selling the wrong thing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if you’re just being enthusiastic about it, if you’re just conveying your enthusiasm, and you can’t control yourself, then you found the right thing to sell.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you’re in the sales business right now and you’re selling something you don’t care about, you need to go find something else to sell.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Let me give you the full text Jeff sent me about your evangelical sales.
speaker: Nivi

claim: Takes you through the bigger picture then makes you want to do the small things.
speaker: Nivi

claim: He’s so passionate—whether it’s a toothbrush, restaurant, or a shitty startup.
speaker: Nivi

claim: If you want to be genuine, if you want to be taken seriously by the people, again, who can see right through you, it’s important not to overthink these things.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Because, for example, if I’m raising money for a company—like so for my new company, Impossible, we’ve raised a round already and we’re probably going to raise another one at some point—the way I approach that fundraising is I’m not driven by an external clock.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Let me put the deck together now and go pitch it.”

I actually wait.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And I measure my own excitement level, and when my own excitement level gets above a certain threshold, then I know I’m ready to go raise the money because now I’m genuinely excited about where the business is, and I feel like the business really deserves another round of financing.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: At that point, it’s not hard for me to go and sell it, and at that point I just have to go find a bunch of people, explain it to them.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if they don’t get it, that’s their loss—I just move on to the next one.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I don’t fixate on the person who didn’t get it, because most people won’t get it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But it’s much more about measuring my own internal excitement level based on the fundamentals of where the business is at.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And if I go out to raise money, that means the business is at the point where I’m excited enough about it that I now just have to genuinely convey what I already see and know to be true.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I don’t have to pitch something that isn’t true.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: I have to just genuinely convey what’s down there that’s getting me excited.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If Naval’s going to pitch you on something, or communicate, or persuade, there’s always going to be a preamble.
speaker: Nivi

claim: There’s going to be a larger context that he sets, whether it’s the problem situation, a story, a historical setting.
speaker: Nivi

claim: If I’m raising for a company, I’ll start raising months and months before we need it—preferably six to 12 months before we need it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: If it’s a kind of deal that’s easy to unwind, if it doesn’t work out, then sure, you can be a little more forgiving about it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A contract is when two parties agree to constrain their future outcome in exchange for working together.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
Then, in exchange, because we’re now constrained to work together in a certain way, we create something of greater value, and we can share the spoils from whatever we create.”

It’s a stag hunt.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So you have to be convinced that this is one of the better paths.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Founders end up getting fired or leaving based on this.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim:
It’s always more like, “I can keep helping you if we can work this out.”

So you’re focused on what you can give them in the future.
speaker: Nivi

claim: The upside is always bigger in the future, and the upside can be 100x, 1,000x, 10,000x bigger.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You’re not looking for the company that gets sold early.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: The same way when you’re doing deals, the most important thing is your time.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: You have to keep your time and your optionality available.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: So if you’re going to give that up, it only makes sense to do that for something that has that opportunity to be 100x or 1,000x on your time or on your capital.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Now, sometimes the big spoils come out and then the knives hit the table, and then you see people get greedy.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Unfortunately, business is war by other means, and so that does happen.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: A lot of times you will get ripped off and you do have to stand up for yourself—sometimes just on principle so that you don’t get taken advantage of in the future.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But generally speaking, I think you want to focus on the upside and you want to preserve your time and your reputation and your mental health—your peace.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But if you feel like, “I can walk away from this and I can recover my time and I know not to work with these people again,” or “I know that these stakes are too small to bother fighting over,” then you just don’t do it.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: Generally, I think people spend too much time splitting hairs on small stakes and not enough time focusing on the big upside.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And that pattern repeated multiple times across other funds that I ran of different types and different structures and different industries.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: But all of that said, I don’t believe in just blindly pursuing something or fighting over something or splitting something or doing some deal.
speaker: Naval Ravikant

claim: And I believe that even if it may not work out the absolute best, it’ll work out fine.
speaker: Naval Ravikant
